title: Arnold Ventures Parole Project
text:
The Council of State Governments (CSG) Justice Center intends to illuminate parole release policies and practices in the United States and how they impact prison population sizes and lengths of stay, using data from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) from up to 45 states to:

Examine trends in their parole-eligible populations;
Conduct original empirical research on racial, ethnic, and gender disparities on the portion of sentences that are served in carceral settings past parole eligibility; and
Conduct deep dives into selected states to shed light on parole board decision-making. For these states, we will seek to identify any disparate decision-making and associated outcomes and recommend changes to policy and practice to prevent unnecessarily long prison stays while maintaining public safety.

title: Missing Data
text:
Questions

Depending on the state, can we assume that people in prison with missing parole eligibility data who have a sentence length of “Life, LWOP, Life plus additional years, Death” are not eligible for parole? Does offense type matter? For example, in the state of Georgia, 30% of people have missing parole eligibility data. Of these people, 70% had a violent offense and 20% had a Life/LWOP/Death sentence length. Of the people with a Life/LWOP/Death sentence length and missing parole eligibility data, 97% had a violent offense.

Depending on the state, can we assume that someone is not eligible for parole because they have a short sentence? Is time served so brief that the parole process might not be deemed necessary? For example, in Georgia, 83% of people with a sentence length of <1 year are missing parole eligibility information.

Do we need to take into account the prison sentencing system of the state to determine who is potentially eligible or not eligible for parole? For example, in Georgia, “While the grant of parole is discretionary, consideration for parole is often not; most inmates are entitled to automatic consideration for parole. In general, this occurs after serving 1/3 of the total sentence. However, at minimum, a person serving a misdemeanor sentence must serve 6 months and a person serving a felony sentence must serve 9 months. Inmates serving 21 years or more, but not a life sentence, become eligible after serving a 7 year term. In addition, inmates convicted of certain serious crimes (e.g. aggravated assault, incest, drug trafficking) are not eligible for parole until they have served 7 years or 1/3 of the prison term, whichever comes first.”
 Word document in Sharepoint for discussing missingness.

title: The Discretionary Power of Parole
text:
In the United States, parole boards hold significant power over the lives of incarcerated people eligible for parole. While 16 states have abolished parole release for crimes committed after a certain date, 34 states still rely on parole boards’ discretion to decide when people can be supervised in the community.
In 2020, there were 88,661 people in prison past their parole eligibility year, highlighting the critical role that parole boards play in determining the timing of release.

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

Sentences  of up to 15 years are subject to DOC time credit that can erode 60-70% of the maximum while sentences greater than 15 years have no time credit eligibility and only parole release is possible before discharge.

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

About one-third of people in prison can apply for discretionary parole. Some people become eligible to apply after serving 1/4 - 1/3 of their sentences. People with sentences of longer than 2 years earn mandatory parole which is early release from prison or jail by accumulating good time, days credited for good behavior while in prison. The law requires Department of Corrections (DOC) to deduct good time from the sentence imposed, one day for every two days served.

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

Arkansas has a bifurcated prison release eligibility process. Some people are automatically “transfer eligible” to parole and are released upon first eligibility unless specific (negative) criteria are met. Other people are “discretionary transfer eligible” and can be released only at the discretion of the Board through the transfer process or through standard parole release. Substantial changes in release law will take effect in 2025 under Act 659 (2023).

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

For most felonies, prisoners become eligible for discretionary parole release after they have served 50 percent of the sentence imposed by the court. This eligibility formula assumes that a prisoner has earned all available good time credits (see below) of 15 days per month.

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

In general, people serving a definite sentence of more than two years where the underlying offense does not involve the use or threat of physical force become eligible for parole after serving one-half the aggregate sentence or one-half the most recent sentence imposed by the court, whichever is longer, less any earned credit. The parole board determines the applicability of the use of force criterion, which imposes an 85% requirement before parole eligibility.

title: Georgia

46%

In Prison Past TheirParole Eligibility Year
Prison Population in Prison PastTheir Parole Eligibility Year
Parole Board Members







Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




By statute, most people in prison become eligible for discretionary parole release after serving one-third of their judicial maximum sentences with some exceptions. Georgia is one of four states that have no system of good-time or earned-time credits that can alter the lengths of incarcerated people’s minimum or maximum terms.






In 2020, there were 11,827 people in prison past their parole eligibility year. This group made up 46% of people in prison for new crimes and with sentence lengths between 1 to 25 years.










The demographics of people in prison past their parole eligibility year reveal notable proportions among Black, non-Hispanic and white, non-hispanic people. Gender distribution indicates a predominance of males over females. Age-wise, the majority of people were 25-34 years and 35-44 years old.
























In 2020, 61% of people in prison past their parole eligibility year were in prison for violent offenses. The breakdown of criminal offenses of people in prison past their parole eligibility year reveals a varied landscape, with the majority of people incarcerated for aggravated or simple assault (26%) and property (19%) offenses.





















In 2020, among the prison population eligible for parole but not yet released, people with sentences between 10 to 24.9 years constituted the majority, representing 66%.






















From 2010 to 2022, the prison population decreased 17%, changing from 56,432 in 2010 to 47,010 in 2022.








Source: BJS Prisoners Series

























Source: BJS Prisoners 2022 - Race and Ethnicity




































From 2010 to 2020, prison releases decreased 16%, dropping from 21,717 in 2010 to 18,298 in 2020.
























Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions.















The demographics of people released from prison reveal notable proportions among Black, non-Hispanic and white, non-hispanic people. Gender distribution indicates a predominance of males over females. Age-wise, the majority of people were 25-34 years and 35-44 years old. These findings provide insights into the populations transitioning back into the community.







































In Georgia, Black, non-Hispanic people are incarcerated in state prison at a rate 3 times higher than White, non-Hispanic people, when accounting for population sizes in the community.

































In 2020 , people in prison past their parole eligibility year collectively spent a total of 5,388 years behind bars instead of supervised in the community on parole. This extended incarceration varied across different racial and ethnic groups.

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

Hawaii has a largely indeterminate sentencing system in which the court sets only the maximum period of incarceration. Thus, when a person has been sentenced to prison the Hawaii Paroling Authority must hold a hearing no later than six months after commitment to incarceration to determine the minimum term of imprisonment and issue a “tentative parole date” or “TPD.” This means that there is often a “minimum” hearing and a “parole release hearing” that are separate. The TPD can later be reduced at the discretion of the Authority with sixty days’ notice to the prosecuting attorney..

Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions.















The demographics of people released from prison reveal notable proportions among White, non-Hispanic and black, non-hispanic people. These findings provide insights into the populations transitioning back into the community.







































In Iowa, Black, non-Hispanic people are incarcerated in state prison at a rate 9.6 times higher and Hispanic, any race people are incarcerated in state prison at a rate 1.7 times higher and Other race(s), non-Hispanic people are incarcerated in state prison at a rate 1.5 times higher than White, non-Hispanic people, when accounting for population sizes in the community.

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

By law, a nonviolent  offender convicted of a Class D felony with an aggregate  sentence of one to five years is eligible for parole review after serving 15% or two months of their original sentence, whichever is longer. The time before the first parole review for all felonies committed after December 3, 1980, is determined based on the sentence being served. For sentences ranging from 1 year, up to but not including 2 years, the time before the first review is 4 months. For sentences ranging from 2 years up to and including 39 years, the time before the first review is 20% of the sentence. For sentences of 39+ years, including life sentences, the time before the first review is 8 years. In the case of a persistent felony offender with a class A, B, or C felony, the time before the first review is 10 years.

Prison Admission Trends

Sentence about change in admissions from 2010 to 2020.







Admission Types

Prison admissions are shaped by two primary groups: new court commitments, or individuals newly incarcerated for a criminal offense, and parole returns and revocations, those who return to prison for failing to meet the conditions of their parole.

In Louisiana in 2020, new court commitments accounted for 76% of prison admissions, while parole returns and revocations accounted for 24%.











Citations
Alper, Mariel E., et al. “Profiles in Parole Release and Revocation: Examining the Legal Framework in the United States.” Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, 13 May 2022, https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/publications/profiles-parole-release-and-revocation-examining-legal-framework-united-states.

United States. Bureau of Justice Statistics. National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables. Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research [distributor], 2022-11-28. https://doi.org/10.3886/ICPSR38492.v1.










How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

Each crime in Louisiana appears to have a statutory maximum. For example, theft of something with a value of $25,000 or over will result in imprisonment of not more than twenty years or a fine of not more than fifty thousand dollars, or both. Any sentence to imprisonment must be “determinate,” in other words, judges sentence individuals to a maximum sentence rather than a range with minimum and maximum limits. Eligibility for parole is determined by type of crime and by criminal history. Unusually, prisoners who have been convicted of non-violent, non-sexual offenses can also age out of the Louisiana prison system even if they are ineligible for parole. If sentenced to a term of over 30 years, a prisoner who has served at least 20 of those years and has reached the age of 45 is eligible for parole consideration. If sentenced to any term without the benefit of parole, a prisoner who has reached the age of 60 and has completed various institutional requirements may be eligible for parole.

In Maryland in 2020, new court commitments accounted for 93% of prison admissions, while parole returns and revocations accounted for 7%.

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

Parole is available to  certain inmates who are serving 6-month or longer sentences and have served more than one-fourth of their aggregate sentence. However, an inmate not serving a sentence for a crime of violence or for certain drug crimes may be released on parole at any time to undergo drug or alcohol treatment, mental health treatment, or to participate in a residential program in the best interest of an expected or newborn child. If serving an aggregate sentence where there is a period of no parole eligibility related to one of the sentences, a parolee can be released when they have completed the greater of one-fourth of the aggregate sentence or the  period of no parole eligibility. For example, if a person is sentenced to 20 years with standard parole eligibility  and another 10 year sentence with parole eligibility after  4 years, they will have to wait five years (20 years * 1/4 = 5 years) to be eligible for release.

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

Misdemeanants with an aggregated sentence of over 60 days and felons are eligible for parole. However, the method of determining release eligibility varies depending on whether an offender is sentenced to state prison (for a sentence of over two and a half years) or to a House of Correction (for a sentence of under two and a half years). Minimum sentences are set by the court, with some limitations. State prison sentences must be at minimum one year in length. For those sentenced to life in prison but given the possibility of parole, judges may set a minimum sentence of between 15 and 25 years. The maximum  term is also set by the court, but may not exceed the longest statutory punishment for the crime of conviction. Good conduct credits earned for work or education reduce  both the minimum term, (for purposes of parole eligibility calculations), and the maximum term, (for purposes of reducing the total term to be served in prison). In general, good conduct credits may reduce a sentence by up to one-third, or 10 days per month.

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

A prisoner must serve the duration of the minimum term imposed by the sentencing court before becoming eligible for parole. If an inmate is serving consecutive sentences, the minimum terms are added together to form the total minimum to be served. Similarly, the maximum terms are added together to determine the maximum possible time to serve both sentences.

In Mississippi in 2020, new court commitments accounted for 89% of prison admissions, while parole returns and revocations accounted for 11%.

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

Inmates who have been sentenced to a definite term of one year or over and who have completed at least 1/4 of their sentence may be  eligible for parole consideration, with exceptions. Inmates with sentences longer than 30 years, including natural life sentences, may be eligible for parole after having served at least 10 years of the sentence.

Prison Admission Trends

Admission Types

Prison admissions are shaped by two primary groups: new court commitments, or individuals newly incarcerated for a criminal offense, and parole returns and revocations, those who return to prison for failing to meet the conditions of their parole.

In Missouri in 2020, new court commitments accounted for 75% of prison admissions, while parole returns and revocations accounted for 25%.











Citations

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

Offenders convicted of class C or D felonies related to drugs, DWI, or other non- violent crimes are generally eligible for release after 15% of their sentence has been served. Offenders convicted of class A or B non-violent or drug felonies, or class B DWIs are eligible for release after 25% of their sentence has been served. If a parolee is serving consecutive sentences, the minimum term for eligibility for parole must be calculated by adding the minimum terms for each individual sentence together; however, this must not exceed the minimum term for parole eligibility for an ordinary life sentence. Several types of convictions make offenders ineligible for parole; these include convictions for first-degree murder,  tampering with a victim or witness, certain recidivist drug trafficking offenses, and conviction as a persis- tent sexual offender. Circuit courts have jurisdiction to grant parole to offenders serving sentences under 2 1/2 years (in other words, those serving non-prison sentences). There do not appear to be guidelines or other criteria for court-based parole release.

Prison Admission Trends

Sentence about change in admissions from 2010 to 2020.







Admission Types

Prison admissions are shaped by two primary groups: new court commitments, or individuals newly incarcerated for a criminal offense, and parole returns and revocations, those who return to prison for failing to meet the conditions of their parole.

In Montana in 2020, new court commitments accounted for 79% of prison admissions, while parole returns and revocations accounted for 21%.











Citations
Alper, Mariel E., et al. “Profiles in Parole Release and Revocation: Examining the Legal Framework in the United States.” Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, 13 May 2022, https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/publications/profiles-parole-release-and-revocation-examining-legal-framework-united-states.

United States. Bureau of Justice Statistics. National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables. Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research [distributor], 2022-11-28. https://doi.org/10.3886/ICPSR38492.v1.










How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

A prisoner serving a “time” sentence may not be paroled until they have served at least 1/4 of that sentence. In Montana, a sentencing judge who imposes a state prison term of more than one year may also impose the restriction that the offender is ineligible for parole and participation in the supervised release program while serving the term. Separate sentences must generally run consecutively. If an offender receives a consecutive sentence after incarceration but before a parole ruling is made on the initial sentence, parole eligibility is determined by aggregating the original sentence and the consecutive sentence. However, if the parole ruling on the initial sentence has already been made, the offender will not be eligible for parole on the consecutive sentence until after the offender discharges the original sentence unless a hearing panel orders otherwise. Due to the fact that an offender may still be eligible for parole on the first sentence, the hearing panel may allow the second term to commence for the purposes of calculating parole eligibility. Note, however, that this does not shorten the length of the consecutive sentence.

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

In general, offenders are eligible for parole upon completion of the minimum term less good time. If an offender has been sentenced to consecutive terms, the minimum term may be calculated by adding together all minimum terms. Parole may be granted only to individuals who have been committed to the Department of Corrections. Thus, all misdemeanants and felony offenders serving a term of imprisonment of under one year in the county jail do not qualify for parole release.

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

Only felons are eligible for parole in Nevada because they are the only offenders sent to prison. A person sentenced for a crime committed on or after July 1, 1995 may be paroled when they have served the minimum term. Each felony sentence has a minimum and maximum term unless a definite term is  required by statute. If an individual is sentenced to consecutive terms of imprisonment, the court must aggregate the minimum terms of imprisonment to determine release eligibility. If an individual is sentenced to concurrent terms of imprisonment, eligibility for parole occurs after the longest of the minimum terms has been served. In some cases, good time may be deducted from the minimum term and hasten parole eligibility.

Prison Admission Trends

Sentence about change in admissions from 2010 to 2020.







Admission Types

Prison admissions are shaped by two primary groups: new court commitments, or individuals newly incarcerated for a criminal offense, and parole returns and revocations, those who return to prison for failing to meet the conditions of their parole.

In New Hampshire in 2020, new court commitments accounted for 63% of prison admissions, while parole returns and revocations accounted for 37%.











Citations
Alper, Mariel E., et al. “Profiles in Parole Release and Revocation: Examining the Legal Framework in the United States.” Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, 13 May 2022, https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/publications/profiles-parole-release-and-revocation-examining-legal-framework-united-states.
Carson, Ann E. “Prisoners in 2020 – Statistical Tables.” Bureau of Justice Statistics, 1 Dec. 2020, bjs.ojp.gov/library/publications/prisoners-2020-statistical-tables.

United States. Bureau of Justice Statistics. National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables. Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research [distributor], 2022-11-28. https://doi.org/10.3886/ICPSR38492.v1.










How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

In New Hampshire, sentences have a minimum and a maximum term. However, if a sentence has a maximum of over one year (i.e. a felony sentence), the judge must also impose a 'disciplinary period' of incarceration of 150 days per year of the minimum term that can then be reduced through credit for good conduct. For example, a three to eight year sentence would have a 450 day 'disciplinary period' added on to the minimum term. If a sentence of imprisonment is imposed, the maximum term may not exceed: life for second-degree murder, 15 years for a class A felony, seven years for a class B felony, or one year for a class A misdemeanor. Some crimes carry mandatory minimum sentences. For example, a second or subsequent conviction for the felonious use of a firearm carries a mandatory minimum sentence of three years. A conviction under the state's version of the Armed Career Criminal Act carries a minimum sentence of ten years. Prisoners may be released on parole at the expiration of the minimum terms of their sentences, plus the disciplinary period of incarceration that must be imposed during sentencing, and minus any credit for good conduct. So, for example, if an individual is given a sentence of 10 to 25 years, they must serve their 10 year minimum and, in addition, 1,500 days (or about four years, one month, and 10 days) of a disciplinary period. However, through good time credit, the inmate can pare the 14 years plus that must be served before parole release eligibility back down to 10 years. Thus, the disciplinary period and good time are designed to offset each other.

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

Most offenders are sentenced to a determined, maximum term of years. These inmates become eligible for parole when they have served one-third of that maximum sentence. However, all offenders must serve a minimum of 60 days before becoming eligible for parole. The court has the discretion to sentence offenders to a minimum term not to exceed one-half of the maximum sentence where it is 'clearly convinced that the aggravating factors substantially outweigh the mitigating factors' in the case. Third and fourth time offenders must serve one-half, and two-thirds, respectively, of their maximum term. The court is also required to set a minimum term between one-third and one-half of the maximum sentence for crimes involving use of a firearm, causing bodily injury while eluding arrest, and repeat convictions for drug possession with intent to sell. Some crimes have mandatory minimum terms that are not discretionary. For example, public officers or employees of the state who commit a crime that 'involves or touches' their official duties have mandatory minimum terms based upon the degree of the crime and proximity to their official duty. New Jersey's parole release statute mandates that a prisoner 'shall be released on parole at the time of parole eligibility,' unless there is a showing by a preponderance of the evidence that the inmate has failed to participate in his or her own rehabilitation, or will violate the conditions of release. The sentencing judge must inform the offender of his or her minimum parole eligibility date as well as what credits can be earned to reduce the duration of the sentence. The Parole Board has discretion to decrease an inmate's parole eligibility period for 'exceptional progress, as evidenced by documented participation and progress in institutional or community educational, training or other programs,' but not below a court-ordered minimum sentence. The Board and the Department of Corrections may also make a parole contract with an offender which reduces the inmate's primary parole eligibility date in exchange for completion of specific programs. If convicted of crimes resulting in consecutive sentences, the terms are added to arrive at an aggregate term equal to the sum of the terms.

Admission Types

Prison admissions are shaped by two primary groups: new court commitments, or individuals newly incarcerated for a criminal offense, and parole returns and revocations, those who return to prison for failing to meet the conditions of their parole.

In New York in 2020, new court commitments accounted for 80% of prison admissions, while parole returns and revocations accounted for 20%.

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

An inmate with an in- determinate sentence may be paroled at “any time” after the expiration of their minimum term of incarceration. The minimum term of incarceration can be reduced through time credits.  Determinate sentences involve a fixed length of prison time with no eligibility for parole release; however time credits can be applied to the sentence and result in early release when 6/7 of the full sentence is complete. This type of early release is called “conditional release,” and is supervised in the same manner as parole. Inmates may be conditionally released by DOCCS, not the Board of Parole. Determinate sentences also normally require a period of “post-release supervision” which is an additional part of the sentence. Conditional release is also available to those serving indeterminate sentences after they have completed 2/3 of their minimum sentence. Presumptive release exists for certain non-violent  offenders who have received a certificate of earned  eligibility from the State Commissioner of Corrections and Community Supervision. This allows inmates to  return to the community under supervision after 5/6 of their prison term is finished, at the discretion of the Board.

Prison Admission Trends

Sentence about change in admissions from 2010 to 2020.







Admission Types

Prison admissions are shaped by two primary groups: new court commitments, or individuals newly incarcerated for a criminal offense, and parole returns and revocations, those who return to prison for failing to meet the conditions of their parole.

In North Dakota in 2020, new court commitments accounted for 91% of prison admissions, while parole returns and revocations accounted for 9%.

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

Inmates in custody of the Department of Corrections and Rehabilitation are sub- ject to the jurisdiction of the Board, unless by statute they are prohibited from gaining parole release. Offenders convicted of a Class A Misdemeanor or higher must be placed in DOCR custody. It appears that unless an inmate is required to serve a minimum term by statute, is subject to truth-in-sentencing laws, or is statutorily ineligible for parole, they are immediately eligible for parole and the Board will consider their case within 30-90 days after incarceration. However, the Board will not consider parole release for those serving a sentence of 120 days or less.

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

Each felony in Oklahoma  has a statutory punishment; there are currently no “classes” or “levels” of crimes as found in other states.  Inmates convicted of crimes committed on or after July 1, 1998 are eligible for parole release after serving one-third of their sentence, unless they are serving a life sentence without the possibility of parole. Inmates serving consecutive sentences are not eligible for parole consideration until one-third of the consecutive sentence has been served. However, Oklahoma has enacted truth in sentencing laws which require inmates convicted of certain crimes to serve at least 85% of their sentence of imprisonment without any reduction due to sentencing credits. Some crimes, such as first-degree rape, have statutory minimum penalties. There are also statutory minimum penalties for second or subsequent convictions for certain crimes.

Prison Admission Trends

Sentence about change in admissions from 2010 to 2020.







Admission Types

Prison admissions are shaped by two primary groups: new court commitments, or individuals newly incarcerated for a criminal offense, and parole returns and revocations, those who return to prison for failing to meet the conditions of their parole.

In Pennsylvania in 2020, new court commitments accounted for 76% of prison admissions, while parole returns and revocations accounted for 24%.

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

When imposing a sentence of partial or total confinement, the court must impose a minimum and a maximum sentence. The court has discretion to set any maximum sentence within the statutory range, but the minimum term may not be greater than one-half the maximum selected by the court. Minimum sentences can be shorter than onehalf of the maximum term, in the court’s discretion, and  can be as short as one day. Inmates in state correctional facilities are eligible for parole release after the expiration of their minimum sentences. In practice, inmates are automatically scheduled for their parole hearing before their minimum eligibility date. The Board may not grant parole to inmates condemned to death, or sentenced to life imprisonment. At the time of sentencing, the court is required to aggregate all consecutive sentences and to indicate the aggregate minimum term to be served, which is not to exceed one half of the maximum aggregate term.

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

 An inmate serving a single sentence that is not a life sentence and who is not classified as a habitual criminal is eligible for parole after serving one-third of the term. Inmates serving multiple terms are eligible for parole after serving one-third of the aggregate time to which they were sentenced, except that inmates serving two or more concurrent terms must serve at least one-third of the maximum term they are required to serve. Inmates already serving one sentence, whether in confinement or on parole, who reoffend and are sentenced for a new crime, are not eligible for parole until they have served one-third of the new sentence (and will not receive credit for the time they served under their previous sentence).  If an inmate is sentenced for multiple subsequent offenses, parole eligibility is calculated in the same manner as described above for an original sentence involving multiple offenses.

Prison Admission Trends

Sentence about change in admissions from 2010 to 2020.







Admission Types

Prison admissions are shaped by two primary groups: new court commitments, or individuals newly incarcerated for a criminal offense, and parole returns and revocations, those who return to prison for failing to meet the conditions of their parole.

In South Carolina in 2020, new court commitments accounted for 93% of prison admissions, while parole returns and revocations accounted for 7%.











Citations
Alper, Mariel E., et al. “Profiles in Parole Release and Revocation: Examining the Legal Framework in the United States.” Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, 13 May 2022, https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/publications/profiles-parole-release-and-revocation-examining-legal-framework-united-states.

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

Inmates already serving one sentence, whether in confinement or on parole, who reoffend and are sentenced for a new crime, are not eligible for parole until they have served one-third of the new sentence (and will not receive credit for the time they served under their previous sentence).  If an inmate is sentenced for multiple subsequent offenses, parole eligibility is calculated in the same manner as described above for an original sentence involving multiple offenses.

Admission Types

Prison admissions are shaped by two primary groups: new court commitments, or individuals newly incarcerated for a criminal offense, and parole returns and revocations, those who return to prison for failing to meet the conditions of their parole.

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

Individuals are eligible for parole release when they have served a certain percentage of their sentence based on the felony class, felony count, and whether or not their crime was a crime of violence. If serving a consecutive sentence, the time to parole will be added together to determine a parole eligibility date. As will be discussed further below, individuals who committed crimes after July 1, 1996 must be released when eligible unless they have failed to substantially comply with their individual program directive while incarcerated. Experts within the state estimate that around 80%  of individuals are released at their presumptive date. The parole date is calculated on admission. Inmates may request review of their established parole date. The Board must provide the inmate an opportunity to present their factual and legal arguments in order to determine the true parole date.

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

Crimes in Tennessee have  both a class (that determines the broad continuum of pun- ishment) that ranges from A through E and a guidelines- based classification (which is a slightly narrower range  within each class). The standard classifications are Range I (Standard), Range II (Multiple), and Range III (Persistent); however, there is also the possibility of an “especially mitigated” sentence or a “career offender” sentence. Statutory law determines the class of an offense, but judges determine the classification of the offense at sentencing, using the  sentencing guidelines. The classification determines both the length of the total sentence and the percentage of the sentence that must be served before eligibility for parole.

Prison Admission Trends

Sentence about change in admissions from 2010 to 2020.







Admission Types

Prison admissions are shaped by two primary groups: new court commitments, or individuals newly incarcerated for a criminal offense, and parole returns and revocations, those who return to prison for failing to meet the conditions of their parole.

In Texas in 2020, new court commitments accounted for 83% of prison admissions, while parole returns and revocations accounted for 17%.











Citations
Alper, Mariel E., et al. “Profiles in Parole Release and Revocation: Examining the Legal Framework in the United States.” Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, 13 May 2022, https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/publications/profiles-parole-release-and-revocation-examining-legal-framework-united-states.

United States. Bureau of Justice Statistics. National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables. Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research [distributor], 2022-11-28. https://doi.org/10.3886/ICPSR38492.v1.










How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

In Texas, the prison population includes two sizeable groups governed by different sets of general rules for parole release eligibility. The lower-severity offense group, a bare majority of inmates, are eligible for release on parole when their time served plus good conduct time equals one-fourth of the sentence imposed or 15 years, whichever is less. The higher-severity offense group, almost half of inmates, become eligible for parole release when they serve half of their judicial maximum sentences or 30 years, whichever is less, and without counting good time credits. Texas inmates may also be eligible for mandatory supervision release, and the Board shall release them, “when the actual calendar time the inmate has served plus any accrued good conduct time equals the term to which the inmate was sentenced.” However, the Board may deny mandatory supervision release to inmates if board members believe that “good conduct time is not an accurate reflection of the inmate’s potential for rehabilitation,” and that therefore “the inmate’s release would endanger public safety.”

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

As the Board explains, “when a person is sent to prison in Utah, the offender must serve the entire sentence imposed, unless the Board acts to release the offender prior to the expiration of the sentence.” The Utah sentencing guidelines are also addressed to the Board of Pardons and Parole, and may help the Board to determine when during the course of the prison sentence release is appropriate. The date of a prisoner’s first hearing before the Board varies greatly depending on the class and nature of the crime committed. The parole eligibility rules for different felony offenses are as follows: for a 3rd-degree felony (non-sex offense), the time before first review is 3 months; for a 3rd-degree felony (sex offense), it is 12 months; for a 2nd-degree felony (non-sex offense), it is 6 months; for a 2nd-degree felony (sex offense), it is 18 months; for a 1st-degree felony with a less than 10-year minimum sentence, it is 3 years; for a 1st-degree felony with a 10 to 15-year minimum sentence, it is 7 years; and for a 1st-degree felony with a more than 15-year minimum sentence, it is 15 years.

Parole Board Members







Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




Most people in prison earn one day of credit for each day served. Credits are deducted from judicial maximum terms to produce earlier mandatory release dates. So-called “indeterminate” sentences carry minimum and maximum terms that are specified in statute and cannot be varied by the court when imposing a prison sentence. There is no standard ratio between minimum and maximum terms. The other sentence type “definite sentences” include terms to parole release eligibility that are set by statutory formula rather than the judicial sentence; the default is parole-release eligibility after 25 percent.






In 2020, there were 1,322 people in prison past their parole eligibility year. This group made up 33% of people in prison for new crimes and with sentence lengths between 1 to 25 years.










The demographics of people in prison past their parole eligibility year reveal notable proportions among White, non-Hispanic and black, non-hispanic people. Gender distribution indicates a predominance of males over females. Age-wise, the majority of people were 25-34 years and 35-44 years old.
























In 2020, 70% of people in prison past their parole eligibility year were in prison for non-violent offenses. The breakdown of criminal offenses of people in prison past their parole eligibility year reveals a varied landscape, with the majority of people incarcerated for property (37%) and public order (18%) offenses.





















In 2020, among the prison population eligible for parole but not yet released, people with sentences between 10 to 24.9 years constituted the majority, representing 60%.






















From 2010 to 2022, the prison population decreased 12%, changing from 6,681 in 2010 to 5,847 in 2022.








Source: BJS Prisoners Series

























Source: BJS Prisoners 2022 - Race and Ethnicity




































From 2010 to 2020, prison releases increased 13%, dropping from 2,970 in 2010 to 3,342 in 2020.
























Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions.















The demographics of people released from prison reveal notable proportions among White, non-Hispanic and black, non-hispanic people. Gender distribution indicates a predominance of males over females. Age-wise, the majority of people were 25-34 years and 35-44 years old. These findings provide insights into the populations transitioning back into the community.







































In West Virginia, Black, non-Hispanic people are incarcerated in state prison at a rate 3.9 times higher than White, non-Hispanic people, when accounting for population sizes in the community.

































In 2020 , people in prison past their parole eligibility year collectively spent a total of 0 years behind bars instead of supervised in the community on parole. This extended incarceration varied across different racial and ethnic groups.

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

Most felony offenders receive indeterminate sentences that contain a minimum and a maximum term. The maximum term may not be greater than the maximum provided by law, and the minimum term may not be less than the minimum provided by law and may not be more than 90% of the maximum term. Offenders with indeterminate sentences are eligible for parole when the minimum term imposed by the court, minus good time earned, is complete. When an inmate begins imprisonment, the Department of Corrections calculates a projected parole eligibility date based on the full potential good time allowance and the minimum sentence. This calculation is not “an award of or entitlement to good time but is merely for administrative purposes.” Inmates who have committed an escape, attempted escape, or assisted others to escape from any institution during a sentence are ineligible for parole on that sentence. Inmates who have committed assault with a deadly weapon on another inmate, an officer, or a staff member during a sentence are also ineligible for parole during that sentence.

title: Georgia
text:
How Parole Eligibility is Determined
In general, parole eligibility occurs after serving 1/3 of the total sentence, and after a minimum of 9 months. Inmates serving 21 years or more, but not a life sentence, become eligible after serving a 7 year term. In addition, inmates convicted of certain serious crimes (e.g. aggravated assault, incest, drug trafficking) are not eligible for parole until they have served 7 years or 1/3 of the prison term, whichever comes first.

text: Time Served

title: Georgia
text: By statute, most people in prison become eligible for discretionary parole release after serving one-third of their judicial maximum sentences with some exceptions. Georgia is one of four states that have no system of good-time or earned-time credits that can alter the lengths of incarcerated people’s minimum or maximum terms.

title: Georgia
text:
In 2020, there were 11,827 people in prison past their parole eligibility date. This group made up 46% of people in prison for new crimes and with sentence lengths between 1 to 25 years.










The demographics of people in prison past their parole eligibility date reveals notable proportions among non-Hispanic Black and White people. Gender distribution indicates a predominance of males over females. Age-wise, the majority of individuals fall within the 25-34 years and 35-44 years brackets.





















The breakdown of criminal offenses of people in prison past their parole eligibility date reveals a varied landscape, with the majority of people incarcerated for aggravated or simple assault (26%) and property crimes (19%). Other offense categories make up smaller proportions of this population.











In 2020, among the prison population eligible for parole but not yet released, people with sentences between 10 to 24.9 years constituted the majority, representing 66 percent.

title: The Discretionary Power of Parole
text:
The Power of Parole Boards
Despite the critical role they play, parole boards are often composed of a small number of individuals. In many states, just 5-10 parole board members are responsible for decisions affecting thousands of people awaiting parole.Parole board members must balance a range of factors, including public safety, the rehabilitation progress of incarcerated people, and the perspectives of victims and their families. These boards often work with limited resources and under significant public and political pressure.

title: Analysis Plan
section: Cost Analysis Based on Illinois Study
text:
Cost Analysis Based on Illinois Study
Study Link
Dynamic Marginal Costs Definition: These costs estimate the financial impact of adding or removing people in the criminal justice system. They include variable costs (directly related to services like food and laundry), step costs (personnel costs), and fixed costs (costs related to physical spaces).

Variable Costs (VC)
These costs change directly with the number of incarcerated people. Examples include food, laundry, and medical care.
Formula: VC = Average cost per individual for food, medical care, etc.
Example:

If food and medical care cost $10,000 per individual per year: VC = 10,000



Step Costs (SC)
These costs change when the number of incarcerated people hits certain levels or “thresholds.” For example, hiring more staff when the prison population increases by a certain number.

Identify the threshold (T) at which new staff or resources are needed.
Calculate the total step cost (TC_SC) for reaching the threshold.
Determine the per-individual step cost:

Formula: SC = TC_SC / T
Example:

For every 50 additional individuals, 5 new staff are hired at $50,000 per year each.
Total step cost for 50 individuals: TC_SC = 5 * 50,000 = 250,000
Per-individual step cost: SC = 250,000 / 50 = 5,000



Fixed Costs (FC)
These costs don’t change with small changes in the prison population but do change with large population shifts. Examples include the cost of the building and administrative overhead.

Identify the large change threshold (L) that triggers fixed costs.
Calculate the total fixed cost (TC_FC) for reaching this large change threshold.
Determine the per-individual fixed cost:

Formula: FC = TC_FC / L
Example:

Building a new facility wing costs $500,000 every 200 individuals.
Total fixed cost for 200 individuals: TC_FC = 500,000
Per-individual fixed cost: FC = 500,000 / 200 = 2,500



Data Needed

Historical Staffing Data: Information on staff changes with prison population changes.
Personnel Costs: Detailed costs for staff salaries, benefits, and pensions.
Operational Data: Records of costs related to running the correctional facilities.
Capital Expenditure Data: Costs for building and maintaining prison facilities.
Administrative Overhead Costs: Costs for running administrative offices.
Debt and Interest Payments: Data on loans or bonds for facility construction or upgrades.
Budget Reports: Detailed budget reports from the WV Department of Corrections and county jails.
Thresholds for Staffing Changes: Specific thresholds at which staffing levels change.



Small Changes (Variable Costs Only)
Formula: DMC = VC * ΔN
Example:

If adding 10 individuals:

Variable cost per individual (VC): $10,000
Change in number of individuals (ΔN): 10
Calculation: DMC = VC * ΔN DMC = 10,000 * 10 = 100,000
Total Cost Impact: $100,000




Medium Changes (Variable + Step Costs)
Formula: DMC = (VC + SC) * ΔN
Example:

If adding 50 individuals:

Variable cost per individual (VC): $10,000
Step cost per individual (SC): $5,000
Change in number of individuals (ΔN): 50
Calculation: DMC = (VC + SC) * ΔN DMC = (10,000 + 5,000) * 50 = 750,000
Total Cost Impact: $750,000




Large Changes (Variable + Step + Fixed Costs)
Formula: DMC = (VC + SC + FC) * ΔN
Example:

If adding 200 individuals:

Variable cost per individual (VC): $10,000
Step cost per individual (SC): $5,000
Fixed cost per individual (FC): $2,500
Change in number of individuals (ΔN): 200
Calculation: DMC = (VC + SC + FC) * ΔN DMC = (10,000 + 5,000 + 2,500) * 200 = 3,500,000
Total Cost Impact: $3,500,000




Cost Savings

Small Changes (Variable Costs Only)
Example:

If a policy reduces the prison population by 100 individuals:

Reduction in variable costs (VC): $10,000 per individual
Cost Savings: Savings = VC * ΔN Savings = 10,000 * 100 = 1,000,000
Total Savings: $1,000,000




Medium Changes (Variable + Step Costs)
Example:

If a policy reduces the prison population by 50 individuals:

Variable cost per individual (VC): $10,000
Step cost per individual (SC): $5,000
Cost Savings: Savings = (VC + SC) * ΔN Savings = (10,000 + 5,000) * 50 = 750,000
Total Savings: $750,000




Large Changes (Variable + Step + Fixed Costs)
Example:

If a policy reduces the prison population by 200 individuals:

Variable cost per individual (VC): $10,000
Step cost per individual (SC): $5,000
Fixed cost per individual (FC): $2,500
Cost Savings: Savings = (VC + SC + FC) * ΔN Savings = (10,000 + 5,000 + 2,500) * 200 = 3,500,000
Total Savings: $3,500,000

Offense Type, Sentence Length  Sources: AOC Data


Any research questions on indeterminate and determinate sentences?
Sentence Type (Indeterminate, Determinate), Sentence Length  Sources: DCR Prison Data - Admissions, AOC Data



Definitions:

Indeterminate Sentences: Sentences that include a minimum and maximum term specified in statute and cannot be varied by the court when imposing a prison sentence. Examples include:

Manufacture Sch. I: 1 to 15 years
Manufacture Sch. IV: 1 to 3 years
Theft over $1000: 1 to 10 years
Sex Assault 3rd Degree: 1 to 5 years
Sex Assault 2nd Degree: 10 to 25 years
Bank Robbery: 10 to 20 years

Determinate Sentences: Sentences where the court specifies a maximum term within an authorized statutory range, but no minimum term. Definite sentences include terms to parole release eligibility that are set by statutory formula rather than the judicial sentence. By default, definite sentences carry parole-release eligibility after 25 percent of the judicial maximum term has been served. Example:

First Degree Robbery: “shall be imprisoned in a state correctional facility not less than ten years.”

Definitions

Survival analysis is a statistical method used to study the time until an event happens, like reoffending after being released from prison. This analysis helps us understand patterns, such as whether older individuals take longer to reoffend or are less likely to reoffend compared to younger individuals.

title: Analysis Plan
text:
Recidivism
The recidivism analysis aims to assess overall rates of re-incarceration, the impact of pretrial detention and bail decisions, and the role of reentry programs in reducing recidivism. By understanding these factors, we can identify barriers to successful reintegration and develop strategies to improve reentry support. This section is vital for addressing the root causes of recidivism and designing interventions that enhance long-term success for individuals leaving the criminal justice system.







Research Questions
Data Sources




What are the overall recidivism rates for different offense types and demographic groups?
Admission Date, Release Date, Re-incarceration Date  Sources: DCR and AOC Data


What are the patterns of recidivism among individuals with BH conditions?
Substance Abuse (SA) Screening/Assessment Scores, Known History of Drug Alcohol Abuse  Sources: DCR Data


How do recidivism rates vary by county? What makes counties with high recidivism rates unique? (Lack of services, parole offices ar far away, high overdose fatality rate, etc.)
Admission Date, Release Date, Supervising County, Release County  Sources: DCR Prison, Jail, Probation, Parole Data, Policy Team/Stakeholder Data Needed

title: Analysis Plan
text:
Day Report Centers
This section examines the role and usage of day reporting centers within the criminal justice system. It covers the types of services offered, the demographics of individuals attending these centers, and overall trends in their utilization. The goal is to understand how day reporting centers function, who they serve, and their general impact on managing individuals under supervision.







Research Questions
Data Sources

Program Name, Program Type, Program Start Date, Program Termination/End Date, Type of Completion (successful, incomplete, removed, transferred to another facility, etc.)  Sources: DCR Data - Program Referrals and Participation

Program Name, Program Type, Program Start Date, Program Termination/End Date, Type of Completion (successful, incomplete, removed, transferred to another facility, etc.), Re-incarceration Date  Sources: DCR Data - Program Referrals and Participation, Releases

title: Launch Presentation
text:
This page provides an overview of the publicly available data that informs the West Virginia Justice Reinvestment Initiative (JRI).

Incarceration Rates and Trends

Adult Imprisonment Rate: In 2022, West Virginia had an adult imprisonment rate of 413 people per 100,000 adults, ranking 20th highest in the United States1.
Prison Population Decrease: The state prison population decreased by 18%, from 7,262 people in FY 2019 to 5,959 people in FY 20231.
Jail Population Decrease: Admissions to regional jails decreased by 21%, from 47,051 in 2019 to 37,184 in 20231.




Supervision Revocations
The number of admissions for technical revocations has fluctuated over the years, with a notable peak around 2020.










In FY 2023, parole and probation technical revocations accounted for 27 percent of prison admissions.












Pretrial Detention and Jail Capacity
More than half of the population (52%) in regional jails in FY 2023 was held pretrial, with overcrowding remaining a significant issue1. In FY 2023, 18% of people sentenced to prison were housed in regional jails, constituting 27% of the average daily population1.













Bail Practices

Cash Bail System: West Virginia primarily uses a cash bail system, which can result in individuals being detained pretrial simply because they cannot afford to pay bail3. This system disproportionately impacts low-income individuals.
Magistrate Decisions: Bail amounts and decisions are typically made by magistrates, who assess the risk of flight and danger to the community. However, there is variability in how bail is set, which can lead to inconsistencies and inequities in pretrial detention3.




Behavioral Health Challenges

Overdose Rates: In 2021, West Virginia had the highest overdose rate in the country at 90.9 deaths per 100,000 people, with 83% due to opioid overdoses. Nationally, the overdose death rate was 28.3 per 100,000 people2.
Substance Use and Alcohol Misuse: In 2021, 16% of adults reported alcohol misuse, and nearly 4% reported opioid misuse, the 8th highest rate in the nation2.
Prison Population and Drug Offenses: The proportion of the prison population incarcerated for drug offenses has remained steady, at 13% in FY 2018 and 12% in FY 20221.




Staffing Crisis and Fiscal Pressures

National Guard Deployment: In August 2022, Governor Jim Justice declared a state of emergency due to extreme staffing shortages and deployed 300 National Guard members to staff prisons and regional jails. This deployment cost the WVDCR $21 million in salary reimbursements and over $2 million for accommodations1.
Correctional Expenditures: The cost of corrections increased 25% between 2019 and 2023, from $363 million to $452 million1.
County Expenditures: In 2019, counties spent approximately $44 million on regional jails1.




Other Critical Issues

Economic Challenges and Poverty: High poverty rates and limited economic opportunities. West Virginia has a poverty rate of 16%, which is higher than the national average of 11.4%4,5.
Reentry Barriers: Individuals face significant barriers to reentry, including difficulties finding employment, housing, and accessing social services. Approximately 30% of formerly incarcerated individuals are unemployed, compared to the national average unemployment rate of 4%6,7.
Homelessness: West Virginia has an estimated 1,348 individuals experiencing homelessness on any given night. Nationally, about 17 people per 10,000 experience homelessness8.
Opioids: The opioid crisis continues to severely impact West Virginia, contributing to high rates of addiction and overdose deaths. In 2021, the state had 90.9 overdose deaths per 100,000 people, with 83% of these deaths due to opioids2. Nationally, the overdose death rate was 28.3 per 100,000 people2.
Employment: Unemployment and underemployment are major issues, with the state’s unemployment rate at 6.1%, higher than the national average of 3.7%5. Many individuals struggle to find stable, well-paying jobs, with a significant portion of the workforce employed in low-wage positions.
Minimum Wage: West Virginia’s minimum wage is $8.75 per hour, which is higher than the federal minimum wage of $7.25 per hour but still challenging for many families to live on. The living wage needed for a family of four in West Virginia is estimated to be $16.28 per hour5.
Medicaid: Many low-income individuals rely on Medicaid for healthcare, with over 27% of West Virginia’s population enrolled in Medicaid. Nationally, about 21% of the population is enrolled in Medicaid9.




Conclusion
West Virginia’s criminal justice system is grappling with several significant challenges, including high incarceration rates, a substantial backlog in regional jails, increasing technical parole revocations, severe behavioral health issues, and a critical staffing crisis within correctional facilities. These issues are compounded by rising correctional expenditures, placing additional financial strain on state and county budgets.



Data Sources
The data presented here is derived from a variety of reliable public sources, including:

West Virginia Department of Corrections and Rehabilitation (WVDCR), Administrative Office of the Courts (AOC), Bureau of Justice Statistics (BJS)
West Virginia Department of Health and Human Resources (DHHR), Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), National Institute on Drug Abuse (NIDA)
West Virginia Judiciary
U.S. Census Bureau, Poverty Rate Data: Link
Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS), Employment Data: Link
West Virginia Housing Development Fund, Housing Data: Link
Council of State Governments Justice Center
West Virginia Coalition to End Homelessness, Homelessness Data: Link
West Virginia Medicaid, Medicaid Data: Link

To do list for Research.


Policy Questions
Questions for the policy team.


Stakeholder Questions
West Virginia Money Topics
Regional Jails:

Does the current population-only formula for baseline jail usage and charges to counties work fairly? Does it create any perverse incentives? What is the cost of current programming, treatment, and education efforts in jails?
How much do counties and the state spend to support the regional jail system? Per diem?
Does the current arrangement of counties to jails fit the different capacities of the jails? (I.e., are the catchment areas the right ones?) Prisons
What is the difference in cost for jails versus prisons and what explains any difference?
What is the cost of current programming, treatment, and education efforts in prison?
What is the correctional officer salary structure for prisons and jails and how does it compare with comparable positions in neighboring states?

Probation & Parole:

Are there pay differences between judicial branch probation officers and state parole officers?
Do counties provide funding for probation budgets in some or all counties and does that create differentials?
How much reliance is there on supervision fees in either system, i.e., what proportion of the cost is covered by fees?
What are per diem costs for probation and parole populations?
What is the cost of the population covered by § 62-12-26, the extended supervision statute for certain sex offenders?

Community Corrections:

How much do counties and the state spend to support the community corrections system?
How many people are served by day report centers? What are per diem costs and differentials across DRCs?
How much reliance is there on fees, i.e., what proportion of the cost is covered by fees?
What would it cost to beef up all DRCs to provide more robust services? To serve more people?
How do other state community corrections acts distribute funds to counties? (I have some slides on this topic.)

Behavioral Health:

How much do counties and the state spend on behavioral health treatment?

How much is aimed at the CJ population?

The dataset has no missing values.

title: West Virginia
text: Most people in prison earn one day of credit for each day served. Credits are deducted from judicial maximum terms to produce earlier mandatory release dates. So-called “indeterminate” sentences carry minimum and maximum terms that are specified in statute and cannot be varied by the court when imposing a prison sentence. There is no standard ratio between minimum and maximum terms. The other sentence type “definite sentences” include terms to parole release eligibility that are set by statutory formula rather than the judicial sentence; the default is parole-release eligibility after 25 percent.

title: West Virginia
text:
In 2020, there were 1,322 people in prison past their parole eligibility year. This group made up 33% of people in prison for new crimes and with sentence lengths between 1 to 25 years.










The demographics of people in prison past their parole eligibility year reveal notable proportions among White, non-Hispanic and black, non-hispanic people. Gender distribution indicates a predominance of males over females. Age-wise, the majority of people were 25-34 years and 35-44 years old.
























In 2020, 70% of people in prison past their parole eligibility year were in prison for non-violent offenses. The breakdown of criminal offenses of people in prison past their parole eligibility year reveals a varied landscape, with the majority of people incarcerated for property (37%) and public order (18%) offenses.





















In 2020, among the prison population eligible for parole but not yet released, people with sentences between 10 to 24.9 years constituted the majority, representing 60%.
